---
title: "Archetypes of the Doom Debate"
subtitle: "Mapping AI Risk Viewpoints — Portrait Framework"
date: "March 2026"
author: "[Author TBD]"
---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Document Meta — read this first"}
This document is a structured draft for a LessWrong post mapping viewpoints in the AI doom debate. The meta notes throughout are **permanent structural documentation** — they explain why each section is shaped the way it is and what to resist when writing. Never delete or shorten them. In this Quarto format, meta notes appear as margin asides when rendered.

**Structure:**

- **§I — Why This Map Exists:** The hook. 3 paragraphs. Creates the problem the post solves.
- **§II — The Underlying Axes:** The scaffold. Five cross-cutting axes that prime the reader before portraits.
- **§III — The Archetypes:** Seven portrait-shaped fingerprints of distinct view clusters. Uniform shape by design.
- **§IV — Three Cuts:** Regroupings of the seven archetypes by mechanism, epistemological stance, and core crux.
- **§VI — What This Map Is Good For:** Brief closing. What the map does, doesn't do, points toward.

**Writing discipline:** All portraits must have inline links. p(doom) numbers must cite where they were stated and specify what definition of doom applies. `[CITATION NEEDED: ...]` markers stay until resolved.
:::

---

## I. Why This Map Exists

::: aside
**META:** Short. 2–3 paragraphs max. Create the problem the post solves — don't summarize the archetypes. Three beats: the frustrating observation → the non-obvious thesis → what this post does and doesn't do. The "but you didn't take a side" objection is forestalled in beat 3. Author locates position in §V.
:::

The debate about AI risk is structurally illegible. Yoshua Bengio and Eliezer Yudkowsky share the exact same threat model. Both believe a sufficiently capable AI system with misaligned goals, acting in the world, would be catastrophic. Yet, one thinks the right response is to avoid building agentic systems, while the other believes that will inevitably fail and demands a highly enforced global treaty. They agree on the danger. They disagree violently on the prescription. From the outside, their debate looks like a disagreement about whether AI is a threat at all. It isn't. It is a disagreement about whether architecture choices can actually close the safety gap.

Surface-level metrics continually mask these structural cruxes. Consider: someone with a 22% p(doom) and someone with a 2% p(doom) can harbor incompatible disagreements, or almost no disagreement at all, depending on which definitions of risk they are carrying. The question "what's your p(doom)?" almost never locates the actual argument.

The debate remains illegible because it is fundamentally multi-dimensional. It isn't a simple continuum running from "not worried" to "very worried" where more information moves you left or right. It operates on several independent, load-bearing axes—such as whether power-seeking emerges by default, or whether current multi-modal models constitute relevant safety evidence. Consequently, individuals who appear aligned on top-level risk are often making entirely contradictory causal claims.

This post draws a map. It doesn't pick winners. A map of a debate isn't useful because it acts as an arbiter—it is useful because it uncovers the structural disagreements, making them addressable. The following seven archetypes and five axes attempt to locate those Cruxes. The framework comes first.

---

{{< include chapters/axes_archetypes.qmd >}}

---

{{< include chapters/archetypes/intro.qmd >}}

{{< include chapters/archetypes/hard-doomer.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** If the Hard Doomer is right, we need a physical moratorium. But what if the danger isn't a single rapid explosion of capability, but a slow, irresistible accumulation of systemic power? Enter the Structural Doomer.
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/structural-doomer.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Structural Doomer assumes bad outcomes are the default result of optimization. But what if the structural danger is real, yet alignment is merely an extraordinarily hard engineering problem rather than a theoretical impossibility?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/conditional-doomer.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Conditional Doomer worries about successfully aligning the system. But what if technical alignment isn't the primary bottleneck at all? What if unsupervised human competition guarantees catastrophe regardless of the math?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/multipolar.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Multipolar Realist sees human coordination as the only check against dangerous racing dynamics. If human coordination is the core dependency, one logical extreme is to preemptively and legally ban the creation of autonomous agents globally.
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/safe-by-design.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Alarmist wants to urgently ban agentic architectures. But what if that entire architectural paradigm is a dead end that will never mathematically reach human-level agency anyway?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/paradigm-skeptic.qmd >}}

::: aside
**META (Pivot):** The Skeptic believes current models won't reach AGI. But what if they actually do, and instead of a sharp capability cliff or an explosion, the transition is just a continuous, mundane process of economic integration?
:::

{{< include chapters/archetypes/gradualist.qmd >}}

---

{{< include chapters/cuts_archetypes.qmd >}}

---

{{< include chapters/utility.qmd >}}
